page: Google Cloud Platform n2-highmem-2 is a memory-optimized virtual machine instance belonging to the n2 server family. It features 2 vCPUs mapped to 1 physical core with 2 threads per core, running on an Intel Xeon x86_64 processor at 2.8 GHz. The instance is configured with 16.0 GB of RAM, yielding a memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8.0 GB per core. It does not include local storage or bundled GPU accelerators, and provides zero complimentary public IPv4 addresses.

Benchmark data reveals distinct performance profiles. The single-core CPU performance measured via stress-ng is 1827.69, placing it in the average tier. However, multi-core CPU performance drops to the bottom 10% with a score of 1875.88, reflecting the physical single-core limitation. Memory bandwidth tests show 102.44 GB/sec for cached small blocks and 13.86 GB/sec for uncached large blocks, both ranking in the bottom 10%. Geekbench scores also align with the lower tier, showing a single-core score of 1099.0 and a multi-core score of 1321.0.

The primary tradeoff of the n2-highmem-2 is its low compute density relative to its memory footprint. While the 16.0 GB RAM allocation supports memory-bound tasks, the single physical core limits parallel processing capabilities, as evidenced by poor multi-core and database operation benchmarks. This instance is suitable for small-scale memory-intensive workloads, lightweight caching layers, or basic database operations that require a higher memory-to-CPU ratio but do not demand intensive parallel computation.
description: Google Cloud Platform n2-highmem-2 is a memory-optimized instance featuring 2 vCPUs and 16.0 GB of RAM, powered by an Intel Xeon x86_64 processor running at 2.8 GHz. It operates on a single physical core with two threads, offering a dedicated CPU allocation but no local storage or GPU accelerators. Performance benchmarks indicate average single-core CPU capabilities, with a stress-ng score of 1827.69. However, multi-core performance and memory bandwidth fall into the bottom 10% due to the single physical core constraint. This instance provides a high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8.0 GB per core, making it qualitatively cost-efficient for memory-bound workloads that do not require heavy parallel processing. It is suited for lightweight databases, caching systems, and memory-intensive applications with low parallel compute demands.
